phrasal_verbs.meaning: appear or happen suddenly or unexpectedly
start doing something in a very enthusiastic way
be in a particular place or state after doing something or because of doing it
start working hard, especially when you should have done this earlier
end someone’s employment, especially temporarily, because there is not enough work for them; stop doing or using something, especially for a short period of time
if something lies ahead, it is going to happen to you in the future
work at different times from usual because you have not worked enough at the normal times
continue doing something in a determined way, despite difficulties, opposition or interruptions
start doing or working on something in order to achieve an aim
if you are snowed under, you have too much of something to deal with
start to employ someone; accept some work or responsibility
help someone to get to the end of a difficult period of time, especially by giving them money until they can get some more
spend time in a relaxed way when you have nothing else to do
end or finish something gradually; gradually reduce work before stopping completely; relax after a period of excitement or worry

job: make the best of a bad job
have a job to do / doing
second: give/take sb a second to do
start: get off to a good/flying/head/bad/etc start

idioms.meaning: used for saying that it is better to solve a problem now, rather than leave it until later when it may be more difficult to deal with
used for telling someone to wait for something and not try to make you hurry

idioms.idiom: at the drop of a hat
idioms.meaning: immediately or in a way that shows that you have no doubts about doing something
used for saying that something happened or existed before you were born or before you lived or worked somewhere
permanently, without the possibility of change in the future
just in time to prevent something bad happening

idioms.idiom: on the spur of the moment
idioms.meaning: if you do something on the spur of the moment, you do it suddenly and do not take time to plan it or think carefully about it

H.title: Phrasal verbs with away
H.instructions: Which of these phrasal verbs with away are connected to the idea of an action continuing for a long time? Write YES or NO.

questions.sentence: Laura was scribbling away at her notebook throughout the lecture.
We decided to break away from the usual routine and try something new.
You can't laugh it away – this is a very serious situation.
He chose to give away most of his collection to a local library.
The team has been chipping away at that mountain of data all week.
Their beloved team captain passed away unexpectedly last night.
She just kept plugging away at the crossword until she finally solved it.
The students have been toiling away on their dissertations for months.
You can hear the printer whirring away in the background.
He's been working away on a solution since early this morning.
